This is the first video in a larger series on reinforcement learning with LLMs. Here I'll give a practical introduction to RL to serve as the foundation for future videos in this series. While this won't be a comprehensive review, my goal is to give you a basic intuition of what reinforcement learning is and how it works. Let's start with the basic question. What is reinforcement learning? And all this is is getting computers to learn things through trial and error. This is in contrast to supervised learning which is what we see most often in machine learning in AI. And what this consists of is we get humans to put together so-called training examples. So these are input output pairs that basically show the computer how to do a particular task. This could be classifying spam emails. It could be summarizing documents. It could be predicting stock prices and basically anything for which you can generate concrete examples for. And then we take these examples and use them to teach the computer how to do things. On the other hand, reinforcement learning is a fundamentally different way of teaching computers how to do things. Instead of giving the computer concrete examples, we allow the computer to interact with the world and get rewarded for good actions and not rewarded for bad ones. So in this case, the computer basically gets to explore and try things and over time learn which behaviors, which actions are good ones to take and which actions are bad ones to take. Comparing these two approaches, supervised learning is very efficient, but it's also quite rigid because it requires us to put together this training data set of input output pairs. So, it requires us to go through this process of gathering data, making sure it's high quality, and then giving it to the computer. Reinforcement learning, on the other hand, can be described as expensive but flexible. Expensive because learning through trial and error takes a lot of time. For example, if you go try and learn calculus all by yourself through trial and error, it's probably going to take you a very long time to learn it. However, if you go to a teacher who gives you worked examples and explains things to you, you'll probably learn the subject much faster. For that reason, reinforcement learning is a lot slower and more expensive than supervised learning. But because we are allowing the model to learn through trial and error and exploring the world on its own, this allows it to learn in a much more flexible way. Okay, so we don't need to create a training data set and we can create a model that's able to do pretty complicated behavior. There have been many landmark successes of reinforcement learning in AI. For example, Alph Go, which learned how to play the game of Go and surpass Grandmaster level. More recently, reinforcement learning has been used for large language models to create these reasoning models. So these models that can think before answering which has sparked the recent wave of AI agents and coding agents that we're seeing these days. Next, let's talk about some basic concepts in reinforcement learning. The first is the so-called agent. This is essentially a decision maker and it lives in a environment. So this is the world that the agent exists in and at any given time there will be some kind of state. This is just information about the environment or the agent or both. And the agent can use this information to execute actions for which it will receive rewards. And the way the agent decides what actions to take is via its so-called policy. So to recap, the agent lives in an environment. It has access to some information about the environment which here I'll call the state. This state is passed into the so-called policy and defines what action the agent should take given this state. Then the agent can execute this action and get rewarded or not rewarded for that particular action. I know that's pretty abstract. So let's go through a few concrete examples which also demonstrate how flexible reinforcement learning can be. The first is consider the game of snake which I played a lot when I was a kid. an old phone that had this game on it and I would play it all the time. But basically, this is a game where food is good, but running into your own body or the border are bad. So, the environment is like this rectangle here and the agent is this snake that wants to eat this dot so it can get longer. The actions available to the agent are that it can go up, it can keep going forward, or it can go down. The state might be the location of the agent's body in the environment. So here I have a basic coordinate system of all the dots that make up the snake's body and then that is just a set of numbers. There will be some rewards in this environment. So if the snake goes over here and eats the food, it will grow and that's a good thing. So that's behavior we want to reward. However, if the agent goes near the border or it goes near its own body, that's behavior we don't want to reward because that will end the game and the snake will die. However, there's no reason why the reward should be plus one for eating the food or minus one for the borders or minus one for running into its own tail. We can set these rewards to be whatever we like. We can make running into the border minus 10, running into itself minus 10, and the reward of eating the dot + three. We can even use a different coordinate system for describing the state. So instead of XY coordinates for each dot of the agent's body, maybe we just have the location of its head and then we have some way of describing where the rest of the body is. So games are a very popular area of reinforcement learning just because the rules are well defined and it's easy to train agents in them. So a lot of the research in reinforcement learning includes playing games like chess or go or Atari or even more modern video games. However, reinforcement learning isn't just restricted to games. We could also conceivably create a stock manager with reinforcement learning. So, here the agent is going to be some stock manager and it might have three actions. It could buy more stock. And then we can give it a reward every time it earns a dollar. And then we can give it a negative reward every time it loses a dollar. And now more recently, reinforcement learning is used with large language models for solving concrete math problems. So at this point we've seen that reinforcement learning can be applied to a wide range of contexts and problems. But how does the agent actually learn what actions are good and what actions are bad? While there are actually many ways we can solve this problem here. I'm going to talk about two fundamental types of reinforcement learning methods. The first are so-called valuebased methods. Here, the agent learns a function to evaluate actions which it can use to decide what actions to take. To make this more concrete, we'll have some function represented by this computer here. And that function can take in the current state and a potential action. And all this function will do is predict the total future reward that the agent can expect to get given its current state and a particular action. So imagine this function is perfect. It will tell us exactly how well a action will pan out in the long run. We can then give it all possible actions that we have access to. So if it's the game of snake, we have those three possible directions we can go in. If it's the stock manager, it can buy, hold, or sell. Or if it's a large language model, it has a 100,000 different actions that it can take. And for any given action, this function will tell us which option has the greatest total future reward. And then once we know this, we can just simply pick the action that corresponds to the greatest future reward. Another class of reinforcement learning methods are so-called policybased methods. Here the agent learns a function to select actions. So with valuebased we're evaluating possible actions but with policybased we're learning a function to select actions. So what that looks like is we'll pass in a state to this function which is our policy and the policy will spit out a action. However, most of the time the policy won't be deterministic. So it won't just follow logical rules like if X happens then do Y but rather it's going to give a probability distribution of possible next actions from which we can just pick the most likely or have some other sampling strategy if we like. The thing that distinguishes valuebased from policy based reinforcement learning is the function that we're learning. So in valuebased we're creating a model to predict the total future rewards through trial and error. While in policybased, we're creating a model that learns decision-making rules through trial and error. Okay.
